NoteIdentifying Storybook Functions






12345678


Which of the following is not the name of a storybook function?

 d06-not-storybook

   hill()        ramp()        steady()        doublings()        recip()         






Match each storybook function to its characteristic behavior. The function osc() …

 d06-osc-behavior


 goes up and then back down to a baseline


 repeats the same pattern over and over


 starts low and levels off at a high value


 increases by the same factor for each unit increase in input








The function hillside() …

 d06-hillside-behavior


 goes up and then back down to a baseline


 repeats the same pattern over and over


 starts at a low value and gradually levels off at a higher value


 decreases quickly at first, then more slowly








The function hill() …

 d06-hill-behavior


 goes up from a baseline and then back down to the baseline


 repeats the same pattern over and over


 starts at a low value and gradually levels off at a higher value


 increases by the same factor for each unit increase in input








The function double() …

 d06-double-behavior


 goes up from a baseline and then back down to the baseline


 the output doubles for every unit increase in input


 tells you how many doublings correspond to a given output


 decreases quickly at first, then more slowly








The function doublings() is closely related to double(). What is the relationship?

 d06-doublings-relationship


 They are the same function with different names


 doublings() tells you the input needed to reach a given output from double()


 doublings() is double() multiplied by 2


 doublings() is the rate of change of double()








Which storybook function would best describe the outdoor temperature over the course of a single day (rising in the morning, falling in the evening, repeating daily)?

 d06-temp-daily

   hill()        hillside()        osc()        double()

 d06-adoption

recip()    d06-pos-recip

 d06-hillside-half

 d06-doublings-8

   -1        0        1        2

 d06-doublings-neg2

   -4        -2        0.25        0.5         





The flat() function always returns the same output regardless of input. What is the rate of change of flat() with respect to its input?

 d06-flat-rate

   -1        0        1        It depends on the input         
















NoteInput and Output Transformations






iiiiiiivvvi


The storybook functions take pure numbers (dimensionless) as input and produce pure numbers as output. To use them with real-world quantities that have units, we use input transformations.
An input transformation has the form \(a(q - q_0)\) where \(q\) is the real-world input quantity. What must be true about the dimension of \(q_0\)?

 d06-input-trans-dim


 It must be dimensionless


 It must have the same dimension as \(q\)


 It must have the reciprocal dimension of \(q\)


 It can have any dimension








In the input transformation \(a(q - q_0)\), what must be true about the dimension of parameter \(a\)?

 d06-a-dim


 It must be dimensionless


 It must have the same dimension as \(q\)


 It must have dimension \(1/[q]\) (the reciprocal of \(q\)’s dimension)


 It can have any dimension








Suppose you want to model oil production using hillside(), and the data shows production reached half its maximum value around the year 1970. In the input transformation \(a(\text{year} - q_0)\), what value should \(q_0\) have?

 d06-q0-oil

   0        0.5        1970        2023         






An output transformation has the form \(A \cdot f(\text{input}) + B\) where \(f()\) is a storybook function. If you want the transformed function to have a maximum output of 100 and a minimum of 0, and the storybook function naturally ranges from 0 to 1, what should \(A\) be?

 d06-output-A

   1        50        100        200         






Continuing from the previous question, what should \(B\) be?

 d06-output-B

   -50        0        50        100         






If a quantity is measured in miles per gallon and you need to create a dimensionless input for a storybook function using the transformation \(a(q - q_0)\), what are the units of parameter \(a\)?

 d06-mpg-units


 miles per gallon


 gallons per mile


 dimensionless


 miles

NoteChapter Eight






1.2.3.4.











Exercise 1 Linear combinations
A café models its daily revenue (R) (in dollars) as a function of hours open (t) (0–12). They use a linear combination of two storybook functions: one term captures a base level of sales, and another captures sales that grow with how long the café is open.
Suppose the model is
\(R(t) = 50\cdot\) flat\(() + 120\cdot\) steady\((t)\).
So revenue equals 50 (dollars base) plus 120 times the number of hours open: flat() is the constant function (value 1) and steady(\(t\)) = \(t\).








1. In this linear combination, what are the two “ingredient” functions?

 fcw-ingredients


 50 and 120


 flat() and steady(\(t\))


 R and t


 dollars and hours






2. What are the coefficients in this combination?

 fcw-coeffs


 flat() and steady(\(t\))


 50 and 120


 0 and 12


 R and t






3. When the café is open 0 hours, what does the model predict for revenue?
 fcw-R0

   0        50        120        170         



4. When the café is open 5 hours, what does the model predict for revenue (in dollars)?
 fcw-R5

   110        550        600        650         



5. The owner considers a different model: \(R(t) = a\cdot\) hillside\((t) + b\cdot\) flat\(()\).
What is the main conceptual difference from the first model?

 fcw-hillside


 The second model has two coefficients (\(a\) and \(b\)) instead of two fixed numbers (50 and 120).


 In the second model, sales grow with diminishing returns (steep at first, then leveling off) rather than at a constant rate per hour.


 The second model allows revenue to be negative for some \(t\).


 The second model uses a different input variable.



















Exercise 2 Multiplication vs. pipelining
For two single-input functions \(f\) and \(g\), two common ways to combine them are:

Multiplication: \(h(x) = f(x)\cdot g(x)\)

Pipelining: \(h(x) = f(g(x))\) (also called piping or composing functions)









1. Consider the function \(h(x) =\) steady\((x)\cdot\) steady\((x)\). What is another name for this function?

 obd-square


 flat(\(x\))


 steady(\(x\))


 The square of \(x\), i.e. \(x^2\)


 A pipeline of steady(\(x\)) and steady(\(x\))






2. Can pipelining two single-input functions ever produce a function with two inputs?

 obd-two-inputs


 Yes


 No






3. Suppose \(h(x) =\) hill\((\) steady\((x) + 2)\). Which function is in the interior position?

 obd-interior


 hill(\(x\))


 steady(\(x\))


 Both are interior


 Neither; this is multiplication






4. In the expression \(j(x, z) = f(x)\cdot g(z)\), how many inputs does \(j\) have, and is this multiplication or pipelining?

 obd-jxz


 One input; pipelining


 One input; multiplication


 Two inputs; pipelining


 Two inputs; multiplication



















Exercise 3 Polynomials and monomials
A polynomial is a linear combination of a few simple building blocks. Those building blocks are called monomials. Using storybook names, the simplest monomials are: flat() (constant 1), steady(\(x\)) (just \(x\)), steady(\(x\))·steady(\(x\)) (i.e. \(x^2\)), and so on. So when we write a quadratic like \(ax^2 + bx + c\), we are choosing a linear combination of exactly three ingredient functions: the constant (flat), the identity (steady), and the square (steady·steady). The “poly” in polynomial means “many” of these monomial terms.
A standard example is height of a ball (in meters) as a function of time (in seconds): under constant gravity, height is a quadratic in \(t\), so we write it in storybook form as \(a\cdot\)steady(\(t\))·steady(\(t\)) \(+ b\cdot\)steady(\(t\)) \(+ c\cdot\)flat() — i.e. \(at^2 + bt + c\). Run the code below to see an example shape (\(2t^2 - 7t + 3\)). You can change the coefficients to see how the curve changes.









1. In the quadratic \(ax^2 + bx + c\), which storybook function corresponds to the term \(x^2\)?

 cow-x2


 flat()


 steady(\(x\))


 steady(\(x\))·steady(\(x\))


 hillside(\(x\))






2. In the example \(2x^2 - 7x + 3\), what are the three coefficients (the numbers we multiply each ingredient by)?

 cow-coeffs


 flat(), steady(\(x\)), steady(\(x\))·steady(\(x\))


 \(x^2\), \(x\), and 1


 2, \(-7\), and 3


 0, 2, and 4 (the domain)






3. The “poly” in polynomial means “many.” Many of what?

 cow-poly


 Many coefficients


 Many inputs


 Many monomial terms in the linear combination


 Many outputs






4. What are the three ingredient functions (monomials) in the quadratic \(5x^2 - 3x + 10\)?

 cow-monomials


 5, \(-3\), and 10


 \(5x^2\), \(-3x\), and 10


 \(x^2\), \(x\), and the constant 1 — i.e. steady·steady, steady, and flat


 Three different storybook functions that are not flat or steady






5. In modeling, polynomials are often used as flexible “modeling clay” to fit curves. What is a limitation of interpreting a polynomial mechanistically?

 cow-mechanism


 They cannot have zeros.


 The polynomial does not tell you why the relationship has that shape.


 They only work for one input.


 They are rarely used in practice.






6. Write the quadratic \(2x^2 + 7x - 1\) in storybook form.















Exercise 4 AM and FM radio: multiplication vs. composition
You’ve probably heard of AM and FM radio. Both send a signal (e.g. music, voice) by combining it with a carrier wave. The two methods correspond to two ways of combining functions: AM uses multiplication; FM uses composition. We model both using the storybook function osc(): a fast osc as the carrier and a slower osc as the signal. Below we walk through both with R.

Step 1: The carrier
The carrier is a rapid oscillation. We use osc with input \(5t\) (five cycles per unit time). In the code, that’s osc(5·\(x\)).









Step 2: The signal
The signal is the “message” we want to send — here, a slower oscillation: osc(\(t\)), one cycle per unit time.









Step 3: AM — amplitude modulation (multiplication)
In AM, the height (amplitude) of the carrier is varied by the signal. We multiply the carrier by a factor that depends on the signal: same input \(t\) into both, then multiply their outputs. So the transmitted wave is carrier \(\times\) (1 + modulation·signal). We use a modulation factor of 0.7.








The carrier’s height rises and falls with the signal.

Step 4: FM — frequency modulation (composition)
In FM, the signal changes the argument of the carrier instead of its amplitude. So we compose: the outer function is osc (the carrier shape), and its input is something that depends on both time and the signal. The wave keeps roughly constant height, but how tightly the oscillations are packed varies with the signal.









Step 5: Side-by-side comparison
Run the two plots below. In AM, what varies with the signal is the amplitude. In FM, what varies is the argument to osc (composition).

















1. In AM, what are the two “ingredient” functions (the two shapes we combine)?

 wtd-am


 Time and frequency


 The carrier (osc at a fast rate) and the signal (osc at a slower rate)


 Amplitude and argument


 AM and FM






2. Which way of combining those two functions does AM use?

 wtd-mult


 Addition


 Multiplication (carrier × a factor that depends on the signal)


 Composition (signal goes inside the carrier’s input)


 Division






3. In FM, which way of combining do we use?

 wtd-fm


 Addition


 Multiplication


 Composition (the signal is part of the argument to osc)


 Division






4. In the FM wave, which storybook function is on the “outside” — the one whose output we actually hear?

 wtd-fg


 The signal


 osc (the carrier shape)


 Both are on the outside


 Neither; FM uses multiplication






5. What is the main visual difference between the AM and FM plots?

 wtd-visual


 AM has one peak, FM has two.


 In AM the wave’s height varies with the signal; in FM the height is steadier but how tightly the waves are packed varies.


 FM is always larger than AM.


 There is no visual difference.

Exercise 1 Many people experience the symptoms of a housing shortage: splitting up apartments to accomodate roommate groups; financial stress due (in part) to high rents; deferring life decisions such as having children; living in deteriorating, aging buildings, and so on.
We’re used to thinking about people in terms of lifespan, but it is also useful to think about buildings that way. City and local governments keep detailed information about the housing stock, largely for tax purposes. Services like <zillow.com> have data readily available to the public.

NoteChapter Two






4.5.6.7.8.9.10.



Exercise 4 Name five units for energy. Remember that “power” has a different dimension than energy.







Exercise 5 Light bulbs generally have a quantity printed on them: the wattage. Similarly, light-bulb sockets often have a “max watts” label advising the user of the largest wattage bulb that can work safely in the socket.

Part a

What is the dimension of a watt?



 bulb-wattage

   Energy        Power        Electrical voltage        Electrical current         











TipAnswer





Since people don’t directly perceive electrical current or voltage, it is hard to form an intuition about them. Instead, training is required to interpret properly the meanings of electrical dimensions.
An exact mathematical analogy to electrical dimensions can be made to water and flow. You turn on the shower and step in for four minutes. As you shower, at each instant you can feel the “intensity” (flow) of the spray from the shower-head. You even have a faucet to set the flow. This flow is analogous to the power used by a light bulb.
Over the full course of your 4-minute shower, six gallons of water goes down the train. Gallons, of course, have the dimension of volume. You used those six gallons over four minutes. Consequently, the flow of water is \(\frac{6\, \text{gallons}}{4\, \text{minutes}} = 1.5\, \text{gallons per minute}\). The volume of water used in your shower session is analogous to the energy used by a light bulb: it is power multiplied by duration. ..id..





Part b

The brightness of the light emitted by a light bulb is proportional to power. Suppose you turn on a 20-watt light when you walk into a room. This consumes energy. The energy used by the bulb is the power multiplied by the duration that the light was on.



If you stay in the room for 1 hour, turning off the light when you leave, how much energy has the light consumed?


 light-bulb-b1

   20 watts        20 watt-hours        Not enough information is given        1 hour         





In terms of the fundamental dimensions—L, M, T—energy has dimension L2 M T-2. What is the dimension of power?


 light-bulb-b2

   L2 M T-1        L2 M T-2        L2 M T-3         





You might reasonably ask, “What does mass have to do with energy?” Perhaps you remember Einstein’s famous equation for energy: \[E = m\, c^2\] where \(m\) is mass and \(c\) is the speed of light. What is the dimension of \(c^2\)?


 light-bulb-b3

   L T        L        L T-1        L T-2        L2 T-2         




Consequently, \(m\, c^2\) has the dimension of energy.

Part c

Consider this statement:



Power consumption is a property of the light bulb, but energy consumption involves both the light bulb itself and the pattern of usage.

    light-bulb-c1

   True     or        False         




Part d

A typical household uses about 10 kilowatt-hours of energy per day. “10 kilowatt-hours” is an amount of energy, but “energy per day” is a flow of energy, that is, a power.



Translate “10 kilowatt-hours of energy per day” into the equivalent watts. For simplicity in the calculation, assume that the energy is used evenly over the 24-hour day.


 light-bulb-d1

   417 watts        10,000 watts        240,000 watts        Not enough information         





Look up the power consumption of a hand-held blow-dryer. When the blow-dryer is in use, energy consumption of the household increases. Which of these is a plausible fraction of average household power consumed by the hair-dryer?


 light-bulb-d2

   10% of average power        50% of average power        100% of average power        200% of average power         









Exercise 6 Perhaps unsurprisingly, the power of an automobile engine has the dimension of power.
Imagine a car with a 100 horsepower engine. What is the equivalent value in units of watts.

 light-bulb-e3


 735.5 watts


 7355 watts

Exercise 7 Chapter 2 quoted a list from Arithmetic for the practical man by J.E. Thompson (1931). He wrote:

Common measurements are of several kinds …. The chief of the common measurements are those of

Length
Surface, or area
Volume, or capacity
Weight, or force of gravity
Time
Angles, latitude and longitude
Temperature, or intensity of heat
Money, or value


Annotate each item of Thompson’s list with the dimension. Indicate whether it is a basic dimension or a derived dimension.

Put basic dimensions here:


Put derived dimensions here:








TipAnswer






Length, L
Surface, or area L \(\times\) L, which we will generally write L2
Volume, or capacity L \(\times\) L \(\times\) L, which we will generally write L3
Weight, or force of gravity M \(\times\) L / T2, or, written another way, M L T-2
Time, T
Angles, latitude and longitude [1]
Temperature, or intensity of heat 𝛳
Money, or value W

Note that four of them (i, v, vii, and viii) are tautologies.
..id..









Exercise 8 The Economist is considered one of the world’s leading news magazines. Here is part of an article entitled “Fear the deficit-populism doom loop” from Aug 24, 2025.

You are a finance minister after a decade of meagre economic growth, shocks from a financial crisis, a pandemic and sky-high energy prices. Public debt is worth more than your country’s gross domestic product, interest rates are at their highest in years and merely servicing outstanding debt is taking up an ever-greater share of tax revenue. Inflation is stubborn. America’s profligacy is satisfying much of the world’s appetite for government bonds, meaning your debt must pay more to attract investors. You lie awake worrying about how to make the numbers add up.


What are the dimensions of public debt? S in units of whatever is the relevant currency.




What are the dimensions of gross domestic product? S T-1




It’s quantitatively incorrect to compare public debt with gross domestic product.

What dimension must the quantity be that multiplies gross domestic product in order to convert it to a quantity that is directly comparable with public debt?
Let’s give the name B to the quantity in (i). You know the dimension of B, but to perform the multiplication you need to choose a value for B. Is there a value for B that is obviously correct? If you say “no,” explain why not. If you say “yes,” explain the reasoning behind its being obvious.




The Economist article continues:

This is the bind facing governments in much of the rich world. The average fiscal deficit in the OECD, a club of mostly rich countries, hit 4.6% of GDP last year, up from an average of 2.9% in the four years before the covid-19 pandemic; interest payments on outstanding debt came to 3.3% of GDP, only just below the amount Nato members hope to spend on defence by 2035. The political-science literature offers some comfort—austerity is not usually a barrier to re-election—but also a warning. Research shows a link between spending cuts and populist success. Indeed, in Britain, France and Germany such parties are already ascendant. Call it the deficit-populism doom loop: ministers face both big deficits and voter revolts, and there is little way of satisfying both the bond markets and the barbarians at the gate.


If fiscal deficit can be stated as a percentage of GDP, what does that say about the dimension of fiscal deficit?




Explain what’s different between fiscal deficit and debt.




Fiscal deficit can be reckoned as the sum of 1) interest payments on the debt (3.3%) and 2) the amount of other spending (on education, pensions, military, etc.) in excess of government income (taxes, etc.) By what percentage would spending need to decrease or taxes need to increase in order to eliminate the fiscal deficit?








Exercise 9 There are several ways to measure transportation services. Let’s focus here on the transportation of people. The distance of transport is, obviously, an important aspect of transportation services. So it the number of people transported.
In a proper measure of transportation services, it seems reasonable that the longer the distance, the more transportation being provided, so long as the number of passengers is the same. Similarly, the more passengers carried, so long as the distance is the same, the more transportation is provided.

Which of the following is a sensible way to measure the amount of transportation?


 prw-base-wlci


 People plus miles


 People per mile


 Miles per person


 Miles \(\times \sqrt{\strut\text{person}}\)


 Persons \(\times\) miles







100 passengers are on a flight covering 500 miles. How much transportation service is provided to the group of passengers?


 prw-2-cksd


 500 passenger miles


 5000 passenger miles


 50,000 passenger miles


 500,000 passenger miles.






Many people are concerned with the amount of fuel used in air travel. For modern jets (with a full load of passengers), the amount of fuel used is approximately 0.02 to 0.03 gallons per passenger mile.

What is the dimension of “gallons per passenger mile?”


 prw-3-kdlw


 L^4 C-1


 L^3 C-1


 L^2 C-1


 L C-1







People in the US are used to measuring fuel economy in “miles per gallon.” This contrasts with the style in much of the rest of the world the unit is “liters per 100 km.” Assume that a group of friends is travelling 600 miles to a concert. They could fly or drive. If there are three friends, how many gallons of fuel will the one-way air travel consume (for these three passengers)? (Pick the closest answer.)


 prw-4-cksd


 15 gallons


 30 gallons


 150 gallons


 300 gallons







Now imagine the three friends are carpooling to the concert in a car that gets 30 miles per gallon. How many gallons of fuel will the one-way drive consume for all three passengers. (Pick the closest answer.)


 prw-5-wels


 20 gallons


 60 gallons


 200 gallons


 300 gallons







Some people reason that it does not consume any fuel for the three friends to fly, since even if they didn’t fly the plane would consume the same amount of fuel. Are you sympathetic to this argument? Explain why or why not.








Exercise 10 Figure 2.1 displays child mortality versus gross domestic product (GDP) on a country-by-country basis. Child mortality is operationalized as the number of children who die between birth and their fifth birthday. It is a “rate” because the number given is a ratio of the number of deaths in this group to the total number of children in this group, but is given in units of “deaths per 1000 children.” That is, the ratio is dimensionless with dimension C C-1 = [1], (Strictly speaking, the rate should be called “Under-five mortality.”)
GDP is often presented as the value of goods and services produced by a country in one year. The quantity shown here is also a rate, described in the label of the graph as “GDP per capita.” The dimension of the GDP is W T-1 C-1. The operationalization of GDP is defined in a way that makes it relatively easy to measure from government and commercial records. All goods and services for which money is exchanged are included. No goods and services for which money is not exchanged are included. Thus, GDP ignores the value provided by, for instance, home child care and home meal preparation. A country which switched from home-cooked meals to fast food would see a sharp increase in GDP but, perhaps, a decrease in nutrition. Paid work need not be productive to be included in GDP. No adjustment is made for losses due to accidents, disasters, or depreciation of buildings and other capital.
Another factor not included in GDP is the price level in each country. Price levels vary from country to country and also from year to year. In Figure 2.1, not only is a comparison made between countries, but also between years. GDP numbers used for international or inter-year comparison are often, as here, adjusted for the “price level” in each country in each year. The price level is determined by what it costs to buy a “market basket” of goods. And that price level varies over the years, a process we call “inflation.”
The GDP per capita presented in Figure 2.1 is adjusted for price level. The adjustment is often referred to as PPP, short for purchasing power parity. The units are 2011 “International Dollars,” a hypothetical currency for comparison between countries.











Figure 2: Child mortality (deaths per 1000) versus GDP for 1960 and 2020. Source: Bolt and van Zanden – Maddison Project Database 2023 – with minor processing by [Our World in Data].




Find two countries (in the same year) that have almost the same GDP but very different mortality rates.




Find two countries (in the same year) that have almost the same mortality, but very different GDPs.




Describe the overall relationship between GDP and child mortality, as best you can discern from these data.

title: Assignment creation notes (QRI-2026)
text:
Notes for creating and editing assignments in this repo. Use this file when building the next assignment (e.g. Assignment 06) so the process is consistent.



Edit only: QRI-2026 repository. Do not modify the parent QR-A repository.
Do not change: Existing assignment files or existing exercise files. Only add new ones.
Read-only use of QR-A: You may read chapter .qmd files, Exer-*.qmd, and Drill-*.qmd in QR-A for content and structure; do not edit them.





Location: Root of QRI-2026, e.g. Assignment-05-CH8.qmd.
YAML: title, subtitle (e.g. “Chapter 8 of the QR-A book”).
Includes (in order): _use_webr.qmd, then R chunk with library(devoirs), library(mosaicCalc), source("_start-up.R"), then _devoirs.qmd.
Drill callout: Collapsible note linking to https://dtkaplan.github.io/QR-A/Drill-XX-<topic>.html. Only add if a Drill for that chapter exists in QR-A.
Chapter block: Collapsible callout “Chapter N”, inside it id <- "aN-chX", then {.panel-tabset} with tabs “1.”, “2.”, … each {{< include _exercises/<name>.qmd >}}.





Location: QRI-2026/_exercises/. Naming: adjective-noun-verb.qmd (e.g. bear-climb-mountain.qmd).
YAML: id (kebab-case same as filename), created, attribution, use (e.g. “Chapter 8 exercise”).
R setup: Optional include=FALSE chunk with library(devoirs), library(mosaicCalc) for stand-alone testing. If exercises use storybook functions, include webr chunk with {{< include _software/storybook-functions.r >}}.
Structure: Wrapper ::: {#exr-<id>} … :::. Comment at end <!-- end of exr-<id> -->.
Answer marking:

MCQ: Use {mcq} block; mark correct option with [correct] (and optional [correct hint: ...]).
Multiple choice / short answer: devoirs_set(choices = c(...), correct = <value>, id = "unique-id").
Free response: devoirs_text("unique-id", rows = n).

Balance: Majority MCQ, some free response. Exercises can be medium length (several MCQs + 1–2 free response per exercise).





Source chapter: QR-A/Chap-08-composing-functions.qmd.
Drill: Drill-08-composing-functions.qmd exists in QR-A → link in assignment.
Exercises: 5, medium length, mix of MCQ (majority) and free response.
Quarto: Do not update _quarto.yml; instructor updates after exercises are finalized.




Linear combinations: (h(x) = a,f(x) + b,g(x)); coefficients; specifying ingredient functions.
Two inputs: (h(x,z) = a,f(x) + b,g(z)); steady/flat; when to use magnitude() or hillside() instead of steady().
Function multiplication: (h(x)=f(x)g(x)) (one input) vs (j(x,z)=f(x)g(z)) (two inputs); interaction term steady(x)×steady(z).
Pipelining: (f(g(x))) vs (g(f(x))); interior vs exterior; single argument only; input transformation (g(x)=ax+b).
Polynomials: linear combinations of monomials (flat, steady, steady×steady, …); quadratic (ax^2+bx+c).












#
Filename
Focus




1
falcon-catch-wind.qmd
Linear combinations, coefficients, ingredient functions


2
otter-build-dam.qmd
Multiplication vs pipelining


3
moth-seek-flame.qmd
Two-input functions / interaction


4
crow-open-walnut.qmd
Polynomials, quadratics, monomials


5
newt-cross-log.qmd
Input transformation, pipelining









Decide which chapter(s) from QR-A to use and read the corresponding Chap-*.qmd and optionally Exer-*.qmd.
Check if a Drill exists in QR-A for that chapter; if yes, add the drill callout with link to https://dtkaplan.github.io/QR-A/Drill-XX-<topic>.html.
Create Assignment-06-CHX.qmd (or Assignment-06-CHX-CHY.qmd if two chapters) following the same structure as Assignment-05.
Create 3–5 new exercise files in _exercises/ with new adjective-noun-verb triplets; do not reuse triplets from this list or from existing exercises (bear, calf, daisy, deer, falcon, fox, moth, newt, otter, owl, puppy, rabbit, spider, squirrel, titmouse, wolf, crow).
Mark correct answers: [correct] in {mcq}, correct = ... in devoirs_set, and use devoirs_text for free response.
Do not update _quarto.yml; instructor will do that when the assignment is finalized.


Last updated during Assignment-05 creation (Feb 2026).

Exercise 1 For each scenario below, choose which storybook function would best model the relationship.









1. Model work productivity as a function of cups of coffee consumed. (No coffee leaves you groggy; 1-2 cups makes you alert; 5+ cups leaves you jittery.)

 bcm-1-coffee


 flat()

2. Model ticket price as a function of movie runtime (minutes). (A 90-minute film costs the same as a 3-hour epic.)

 bcm-2-movie


 flat()


 steady()

 hill()

3. Model tide height as a function of hour of the day (0-24) at a coastal beach.

 bcm-3-tide


 flat()

 hill()

 bcm-4-language

5. Model water pressure on a diver as a function of depth below ocean surface (meters).

6. Model times a meme is shared per day as a function of days since it first appeared. (Memes explode in popularity, peak, then fade.)

 bcm-6-meme

Reflection:
7. Choose ONE of the scenarios above and make a case for using a different storybook function to model it. Is the alternative better, equivalent, or worse than your original answer? Why?















Exercise 2 The storybook functions double() and doublings() are related. Use the R chunk to explore them.









What is double(5)?

 frb-double-5

   5        10        16        25        32         




What is double(-3)?

 frb-double-neg

   -8        -3        0.125        0.25        3         




What is doublings(64)?

 frb-doublings-64

   4        5        6        7        8         




What is doublings(0.25)?

 frb-doublings-quarter

   -4        -2        -1        0.25        2         




If double(x) outputs 256, what is x?

 frb-find-input

   4        6        8        16        128         




If doublings(x) outputs 5, what is x?

 frb-find-input-2

   5        10        16        25        32         




What is doublings(double(4))?

 frb-compose-1

   2        4        8        16        256         




What is double(doublings(64))?

 frb-compose-2

   6        12        32        64        4096         




Based on questions 7 and 8, what is the relationship between double() and doublings()?


 frb-inverse


 They always give the same output


 They are inverses—each undoes the other


 One is always twice the other


 They have no particular relationship



















Exercise 3 Both recip(x) and double(-x) produce outputs that decrease as the input increases. But they model fundamentally different processes. This exercise will help you discover the difference.








Scenario A — Sharing Pizza
A pizza has 24 slices. The more people who share it, the fewer slices each person gets.



People
Slices per person




1
24


2
12


3
8


4
6


6
4


8
3



Scenario B — Radioactive Decay
A sample starts with 100 grams of a radioactive isotope. Each year, exactly half of the remaining material decays.



Years passed
Grams remaining




0
100


1
50


2
25


3
12.5


4
6.25




Part 1: Noticing the Pattern

In Scenario A (pizza), if the number of people doubles from 3 to 6, what happens to slices per person?


 sgn-pizza-double

   It doubles        It is cut in half        It decreases by 4        It stays the same         





In Scenario A (pizza), if the number of people increases by 1 (from 3 to 4), what happens to slices per person?


 sgn-pizza-add-one

   It is cut in half        It decreases by 2        It decreases by a factor of 4/3        It stays the same         





In Scenario B (radioactive decay), if you wait one more year (from year 2 to year 3), what happens to the amount remaining?


 sgn-decay-one-more

   It doubles        It is cut in half        It decreases by 12.5        It decreases by 25         





In Scenario B (radioactive decay), if you wait two more years (from year 1 to year 3), what happens to the amount remaining?


 sgn-decay-two-more

   It is cut in half        It is cut to one quarter        It decreases by 37.5        It is cut to one third         





Part 2: The Key Distinction

In Scenario A, what causes the output to be cut in half?


 sgn-trigger-a


 Doubling the input


 Adding 1 to the input


 Waiting one time period


 Subtracting half from the output







In Scenario B, what causes the output to be cut in half?


 sgn-trigger-b


 Doubling the input


 Adding 1 to the input


 Multiplying the input by itself


 Subtracting half from the output







Which statement best captures the difference between recip and double(-x)?


 sgn-key-difference


 recip decreases and double(-x) increases


 recip is for small numbers, double(-x) is for large numbers


 recip: “doubling input halves output” vs double(-x): “each unit of input halves output”


 They are mathematically the same function







Part 3: Apply Your Understanding
For each scenario, decide which type of relationship it represents.

Splitting a bill: If 3 friends split a $90 bill, each pays $30. If 6 friends split it, each pays $15.


 sgn-apply-bill

   recip        double(-x)         





Medicine metabolism: A drug’s concentration drops by half every 4 hours.


 sgn-apply-medicine

   recip        double(-x)         





Travel time: Driving 120 miles at 30 mph takes 4 hours. At 60 mph it takes 2 hours. At 120 mph it takes 1 hour.


 sgn-apply-travel

   recip        double(-x)         





Depreciation: A car loses 15% of its current value each year.


 sgn-apply-depreciation

   recip        double(-x)         





Resolution: A printer’s ink cartridge can print 1000 pages. If you print 250 photos (each uses 4 pages worth of ink), you can print 0 photos. But if you print smaller photos using 2 pages of ink each, you can print 500.


 sgn-apply-printer

   recip        double(-x)         

















Exercise 4 This exercise focuses on input and output transformations—the technique for adapting storybook functions to model real-world data.
The setup: You’re modeling the spread of a viral video. The data shows:

At week 0, essentially no one had seen it (views ≈ 0)
By week 10, about half of the eventual maximum viewers had seen it
By week 20, views had essentially plateaued at 2 million

The hillside() function naturally outputs ≈ 0 for large negative inputs, 0.5 when input = 0, and ≈ 1 for large positive inputs.








Part A: Input transformation
The input transformation has the form \(a(q - q_0)\) where \(q\) is the real-world input (weeks).

What is the horizontal shift \(q_0\)? (At what week should hillside() output 0.5?)

 rpg-q0

   0        0.5        5        10        20         




We want hillside() to output ≈ 1 around week 20 and ≈ 0 at week 0. For the output to be nearly 1, the input to hillside() should be around +5. Given \(q_0 = 10\), solve for \(a\) in: \(a(20 - 10) = 5\)

 rpg-a

   0.1        0.2        0.5        1        2         




What are the units of \(a\)?


 rpg-a-units

   weeks        per week        views        dimensionless         




Part B: Output transformation
The output transformation has the form \(A \cdot\) hillside(···) \(+ B\).

What is the vertical scaling \(A\)? (What value scales the output from 0–1 to 0–2,000,000?)

 rpg-A

   1        1000        1e+06        2e+06         




What are the units of \(A\)?


 rpg-A-units

   weeks        per week        views        dimensionless         




Part C: Build and test the model

Define your transformation parameters and plot the model. Fill in the values for a, q0, and A.

In R, you define a variable with <- (the assignment arrow). For example: x <- 5 stores the value 5 in a variable named x.









According to your model, approximately how many views would there be at week 5?

 rpg-week5

   about 13,000        about 150,000        about 500,000        about 1,000,000         




Model limitations: What aspects of real viral video spread does this model fail to capture? Consider factors like the initial launch, word-of-mouth dynamics, platform algorithms, or what happens after the “plateau.”
















Exercise 5 Time-of-Use Electricity Pricing
Your electric utility charges different rates depending on the time of day. You want to model the price so you can decide when to charge your electric vehicle, run the dishwasher, or do laundry.
The utility publishes sample rates at several times of day:



Time of day
Price ($/kWh)




4 AM
$0.08 (lowest)


10 AM
$0.20


4 PM
$0.32 (highest)


10 PM
$0.20



Assume the price changes continuously and smoothly throughout the day (not in discrete steps).








Your task: Build a model for electricity price (in $/kWh) as a function of hour of the day (0-24).
Output transformation: The output transformation scales and shifts the vertical axis.

What is the vertical shift \(Q_0\) (the average price, halfway between highest and lowest)?

 ows-avg

   0.08        0.12        0.2        0.24        0.32         




What is the amplitude \(A\) (how far the price swings above or below the average)?

 ows-amp

   0.08        0.12        0.2        0.24        0.32         



Input transformation: The input transformation scales and shifts the horizontal axis.

The basic osc() has a period of 1. To make the model have a period of 24 hours, what goes inside osc()?

 ows-period

   hour/24        hour*24        hour - 24        24/hour         




The price peaks at 4 PM (hour 16), but osc() peaks when its input equals 0.25. What horizontal shift \(q_0\) aligns the peak correctly?

 ows-shift

   (hour - 10)/24        (hour + 10)/24        (hour - 4)/24        (hour + 4)/24         



The complete model:

Write the complete model for price as a function of hour (0-24):




Decision time: Your EV needs 4 hours to charge and uses about 10 kWh. According to your model, approximately how much would it cost to charge starting at midnight vs. starting at 5 PM?




Model validity: In reality, most power companies use step changes (e.g., “off-peak” from 9 PM to 7 AM at $0.10, “peak” from 4 PM to 9 PM at $0.30) rather than continuously varying rates. Does this information significantly change the validity of your model for making decisions about when to run appliances? Explain.
















Exercise 6 Chemical Spill Response
A tanker truck accident has spilled industrial solvent into a municipal reservoir. The city immediately shut off water supply and began monitoring contamination levels.
The EPA safe limit for this chemical is 5 parts per million (ppm). Initial readings show a concentration of 80 ppm. The chemical degrades through a combination of natural processes: volatilization (evaporation), photolysis (breakdown by sunlight), and dilution from incoming fresh water.
Environmental engineers take measurements over the following weeks:



Days after spill
Concentration (ppm)




0
80


7
40


14
20


21
10



The city council needs to know: When will the water be safe to drink?








Your task: Build a model for contamination level (in ppm) as a function of days since the spill, then use it to answer the city’s question.
Your model:
Write a formula for concentration C as a function of days d:


The decision:
According to your model, on what day will the concentration first drop below the 5 ppm safety threshold? Show your reasoning.


Reflection:
The mayor wants to announce a reopening date to the public. What date would you advise, and why might you recommend waiting longer than what your model strictly predicts? Consider factors like measurement uncertainty, model limitations, and the consequences of being wrong.

NoteDrill for Chapter 10 (Hypothesis and likelihood / Bayes)





Open drill questions in this document
Remember to submit your drill answers through that document. But for the exercises in the present document, use the “collect answers” at the top of this page.









NoteChapters 7, 9, and 10 — Exercises





These exercises draw on uncertainty in quantities (Ch 7), likelihood (Ch 9), and Bayesian updating (Ch 10).

1.2.3.



Exercise 1 You’re trying to predict the outcome of a football game, updating your predictions as the game proceeds. The competing hypotheses here are the possible end scores for your team: 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, …, 100, 100+. I don’t know which is your team and what are your beliefs about it, so I can’t tell you much about what relative probability you assign to each of these 100 possible outcomes, but you have your own views, well informed or not.
Purely for the sake of simplicity, let’s consider just these 20 hypotheses: the scores 5 through 24, with equal relative probability assigned to each: 1.
A. You don’t need to translate this relative probability distribution into an (absolute) probability, but if you were to do so, what would it be?

 jsp-A-3982


 0.10 for each of the odd-valued scores.


 1% for score of 5, increasing linearly to 13.5% for score 24.


 0.05 for each of the hypotheses.


 Not enough information has been given.






Now it’s half-time. Your team has a score of 10 pts. To update your before-the-game opinion, you need to multiply that relative probability by a likelihood function: What is the probability of the half-time outcome given each of the hypotheses.
B. Some of the hypotheses can be assigned zero likelihood given the half-time observation. Which are these?

 jsp-B-3843


 Hypothethetical ending scores of 5 through 9 have zero likelihood.


 Hypothetical ending score of 5 through 10 have zero likelihood.


 Anything can happen in a football game. Best not to assign zero likelihood to any before-the-game hypothesis.


 There is no such thing as zero likelihood.






C. What’s a better name for relative probability distribution that encodes your “before-the-game opinion” on your team’s score?

 jsp-C-k3ks

   Posterior        Prior        Likelihood        Antebellum        Frequency         




D. What’s a proper name for your updated distribution of relative probabilities taking into account the half-time score?

 jsp-D-3l2s

   Posterior        Prior        Likelihood        Antebellum        Frequency         









Exercise 2 Consider a bad movie plot organized along these lines: The protagonist has a disturbing dream that the universe has changed from “Normal” to a new state: “Doom.” Doom was hitherto thought impossible. Doom and Normal constitute two hypotheses about the state of the universe.
To say that a hypothesis is impossible is equivalent to assigning it probability zero. That’s what everyone else believes, but our protagonist, on account of his dream, assigns Doom the rhetorical odds “one-in-a-million.”

If an outcome has probability zero, what are the odds of that outcome?


 des-1-ckd


 Zero


 One


 Infinity


 You can’t use odds here.






Now, in typical movie fashion, the protagonist starts to observe events—call them “strange events”—that shouldn’t be happening. In the Doom universe, such events have a high likelihood. But in the normal universe they could also be observed due to optical illusions, distraction, or tricks played by the neighborhood children who think the protagonist is crazy. Let’s assign each observed strange event a likelihood ratio of 2 in favor of Doom.

Explain what feature of Bayesian reasoning allows evidence to be in favor of a hypothesis that we believe is impossible.




Translate a likelihood ratio of 3 according to the verbal scale for evidence:


 des-3-lfs


 No evidence


 Limited


 Moderate


 Moderate to strong






The Bayesian updating calculation has a particularly simple mathematical form when the prior probability is stated in terms of odds.
\[\text{posterior odds} = \text{prior odds} \times \text{likelihood ratio} .\]
For the character who claims that the prior odds of Doom is zero, the posterior odds must always remain zero regardless of the likelihood ratio. In the movies, such a character always ends up dying in a dramatic way, just punishment for his skepticism.

For our protagonist, who started with a prior odds of one-in-a-million, the posterior odds after one such “strange” event will be what?


 des-4-sot


 One in a million


 Two in a million


 One in one-hundred thousand


 One in ten thousand






Our protagonist starts to encounter other “strange” reports, hearing about them on the radio, seeing them in news reports from diverse locations, and so on. For the skeptic, these are all of no import. It is impossible to move the skeptic off his prior odds of zero. But our protagonist takes them seriously. Or, at least, he is willing to fold them in to a Bayesian calculation.

After five strange events (in total), what should be our protagonist’s posterior odds? (Pick the closest answer.)


 des-5-hjw

   0.000001        0.00003        0.0001        0.003        0.01        0.3         





Another five strange events are observed, making 10 in all. What should be our protagonists posterior odds?


 des-6-yqe

   0.000001        0.00003        0.0001        0.003        0.01        0.3         









Exercise 3 Prior and posterior: the ICBM-style example (from class and the book)
This exercise revisits the example from class and the book in which we learn an unknown probability of success (p) by starting with a prior distribution and updating with data to get a posterior distribution.
We use a uniform prior: before seeing any data, we treat every value of (p) between 0 and 1 as equally plausible. When we observe (k) successes in (n) trials, Bayesian updating gives a posterior distribution: the density is highest where the data suggest (p) is likely to be. The MLE (maximum likelihood estimate) is (k/n), the fraction of successes—shown as a red dashed line on the plots below.
The figures below are pre-generated. Use them to answer the questions; you do not need to understand or run any code.
Part 1: Flat prior, then 4 consecutive successes
The figure below shows the prior (no data) and then the posterior after 1 success in 1 try, 2 in 2, 3 in 3, and 4 in 4. The red dashed line in each posterior plot is the MLE ((k/n)).








Figure 1: Prior (no data) and posteriors for 1/1, 2/2, 3/3, 4/4 successes





Part 2: Informative prior from past data, then 4 new successes
Sometimes we already have past data. Suppose we had 7 successes in 16 trials and we use that to form our prior (no longer flat). Then we run 4 more trials and get 4 successes (11 successes in 20 trials total). The figure below shows the prior (based on 7/16) and the posterior after updating with the new data (11/20).








Figure 2: Prior from 7 successes in 16 trials; posterior after 4 more successes (11/20 total)





Comprehension questions
1. In Part 1, the first plot is “Prior (no data).” What does that plot represent?

 hcf-prior


 The probability of success after we see the data.


 Our uncertainty about the probability of success before we see any data.


 The likelihood of the data given each value of p.


 The number of successes we expect in the next trial.






2. As we go from 1/1 to 2/2 to 3/3 to 4/4 in Part 1, what happens to the posterior?

 hcf-sharpen


 It stays flat (uniform).


 It becomes more concentrated (narrower) and shifts toward the observed success rate.


 It becomes more spread out.


 It always peaks at 1.






3. What does the red dashed line in the posterior plots represent?

 hcf-mle


 The prior mean.


 The posterior mean.


 The maximum likelihood estimate (MLE), i.e. k/n.


 The number of trials.






4. In Part 2, we use a prior based on 7 successes in 16 trials, then see 4 more successes in 4 trials (11/20 total). Why is the posterior in Part 2 not as concentrated near 1 as the “4 successes in 4 trials” posterior in Part 1?

 hcf-informative


 Because we used a different prior; the earlier 7/16 pulls the posterior away from 1 and reflects that we have mixed evidence overall (11/20 = 0.55).


 Because we ran fewer trials in Part 2.


 Because we have more total trials in Part 2, so the distribution is wider.


 Because the MLE is different.






5. If we had started with a uniform prior and then observed 11 successes in 20 trials, we would get a posterior centered near 11/20. How would that posterior compare to the one in Part 2 (which used a 7/16 prior then 11/20)?

 hcf-compare


 They would be identical.


 The uniform-prior posterior would be more concentrated near 11/20, since we didn’t “mix in” the earlier 7/16.


 The uniform-prior posterior would be flatter.


 The Part 2 posterior would always be to the right of the uniform-prior posterior.

title: About
text:
At the University of Austin, our students pursue a Bachelor of Arts in Liberal Studies. As part of their required course of study, students must complete a core curriculum called the Intellectual Foundations, which consists of 15 intensive courses that introduce students to the various languages of understanding and teach them to see things whole. As part of this sequence, students are challenged to confront timeless questions beginning in antiquity and culminating in the present day.
There are two quantitative reasoning courses in the Intellectual Foundation curriculum. These are:

Quantitative Reasoning I (“QR — Analysis”), which teaches students the mathematical modeling tools useful for modelling real-world phenomena.
Quantitative Reasoning II (“QR — Data”), which teaches students to extract useful information from data using modern data science techniques.

Both QR — Analysis and QR — Data are unlike the introductory courses found at almost all other colleges and universities. We have selected topics based on their utility in modern professional work. In contrast, basic topics in the traditional curriculum reflect the historical development of mathematics rather than the needs of modern work. Modern topics widely used in the economy and scientific/technical worlds are relegated to so-called “advanced” courses far downstream where they are inaccessible to almost all students.
Our two QR courses dramatically streamline the pre-requisites for successful work with modern quantitative topics. Surprisingly often the “advanced” topics are readily accessible to students, so long as one is willing to move beyond traditional pedagogy and avoid unwarranted devotion to topics that were important historically but are no longer necessarily for understanding.

The benchmark figure is how many watts per kilo a cyclist can sustain for a one-hour effort. Every rider now has a power meter fitted to their bike, so they know their numbers in a constant, real-time way (together with heart rate, speed, and other measurements).

The article describes the then-current leader in the Tour de France as having a power-to-weight ratio of seven watts per kilogram.

Exercise 2 Consider an everyday kind of question: Which is more to blame for discomfort in hot weather, the temperature or the humidity?
The blame question makes sense only if we think that humidity and temperature have something to do with comfort. Everyday experience strongly suggests that they do. For quantitative understanding, we can translate “something to do with …” into a mathematical framework: that comfort is a function of humidity and temperature. Functions are not the only way to represent the idea of “something to do with,” but experience has shown that they are powerful ways to do so. Knowing how functions are constructed and how to extract information from them is an essential component of quantitative analysis.

The left part of the tilde expression is a formula written in more or less conventional algebraic form. Other examples for this formula: 2*z + 3, osc(z), hillside(z) * osc(z).
The right part of the tilde expression gives the name of the input variable. In the example, the name is z, but you can use whatever name you think appropriate. Note that the formula in (i) must be written in terms of the input-variable name.
The middle part of the tilde expression is merely punctuation, but it is essential punctuation. Among other things, the tilde makes clear what are the left and right sides of the tilde expression.

Exercise 6 The slice_plot() function (see Exercise 3) produces the graph of a function with one input. Convention calls for the input to correspond to the horizontal axis and the output to the vertical axis.
For functions with two inputs, the input corresponds to the points in a two-dimensional space. That is, each possible input is a coordinate pair. To graphically represent each possible input uniquely, we need to use both the horizontal and vertical axes, one for the first input and the other for the second input. Where, then, to show the output.
Our preferred graphical format for functions of two inputs is called a ☞ contour plot ☜. The R chunk below shows the R/mosaic code to generate a plot. Run the chunk.








This R expression follows a similar form to that used for slice_plot(). Notice, however, that the tilde expression used in the first argument names both inputs: x & y on the right-hand side of the tilde. Likewise, the definition of the graphics domain involves two components, one for each of the inputs.
It takes some practice to get comfortable reading contour plots. The labels along each blue ☞ contour ☜ specify the output for any input that lies on the contour.
In principle, each possible input point has some contour line running through it. This is the same as saying that for each input, say, (x=1, y=2), the function has an output value. But if we drew a contour for every output value, the graph would be filled with blue ink and incomprehensible. To avoid this problem, only a handful of contours are drawn. Also, to avoid cluttering up the graph with output value labels, the contour_plot() R function labels only every second contour. The output value for the unlabeled contours are half-way in-between the output values for the two labeled contours that run side-by-side with the unlabeled one.
To illustrate, the input point (x=1, y=2) falls about half-way between the contour labeled 0.6 and the unlabeled contour corresponding to output value 0.4. Since the input point is located between the 0.4 and 0.6 contours, the corresponding output will be approximately 0.5.
In this book, we won’t dwell on teaching the syntax of the contour_plot() function. When we need it, we will give you a template so that you just have to fill in the details: the names of the two inputs, the function itself, and the extent of the graphics domain along each axis.
